data.title: A Jet to Caracas Becomes the Face of Trump's Venezuela Reset — WSJ
data.description:
By Vera Bergengruen

CARACAS, Venezuela — An American Airlines jet emblazoned with America's 250th-anniversary logo landed here Thursday, completing the first direct commercial flight between the U.S. and Venezuela since 2019 and capping a day of celebration in Miami and Caracas.

The 76-seat American Eagle flight was hailed as the most visible symbol yet of President Trump's rapid normalization of ties with Venezuela, with officials from both countries characterizing the resumption of direct travel as a milestone.

"Direct flights are back," said John Barrett, who recently took up his posting as the top American diplomat in Caracas. "Commerce is flowing. Venezuela's economic engine is ramping up, and we're just getting started."

The festivities began before American Flight 3599 took off from Miami International Airport. At the gate, Venezuelan and U.S. officials, lawmakers, Miami influencers and journalists mingled over cafecitos and arepas as a DJ played reggaeton. Airport workers in vests waved Venezuelan flags as officials and airline executives cut a ribbon.

After the three-hour flight — which at $1,700 round-trip remains out of the reach of most Venezuelans in Miami or their homeland — passengers were greeted by another round of celebrations in Caracas. Speaking at the Simón Bolivar International Airport outside Caracas, American officials said the flight signaled that Venezuela was open for business.

"We are moving at Trump speed to get things done," said Jarrod Agen, a White House energy adviser and director of the National Energy Dominance Council, a body Trump set up to boost energy projects.

The day's events would have been unthinkable four months ago, when the U.S. military bombed Caracas and captured Venezuelan strongman Nicolás Maduro and his wife in an overnight raid. After putting Maduro's vice president, Delcy Rodríguez, in his place, the Trump administration embarked on an unusual arrangement to attract American investors to Venezuela while solidifying ties between the two governments. The effort has assigned higher priority to stability and oil and mining deals than to a democratic transition, leaving Maduro's political machinery intact.

The photo-ops underscored the whiplash of the past four months. Venezuela's Ambassador to the U.S., Félix Plasencia, who previously served as Maduro's foreign minister, stood shoulder-to-shoulder with Miami officials who have long denounced the regime he served. U.S. officials spoke near portraits of Maduro that still hang in the Caracas airport.

"This flight marks the transition from dialogue to action," Plasencia declared in a speech outside the gate in Miami, where a screen showed images of waterfalls and beaches to promote Venezuelan tourism. "We're witnessing one of the most concrete, tangible, and verifiable expressions of mutual recognition in this new phase of bilateral relations."

But an uneasy tension festered behind the fanfare.

"What many Venezuelans here in Miami-Dade truly want — what they dream of — is the day they can fly to a free Venezuela," Miami-Dade Mayor Daniella Levine Cava said, "where democracy is restored, the will of the people is respected [and] institutions are strengthened."

The newfound ties between the Trump administration and Rodriguez's government don't sit well with many Venezuelans, who in a poll this year overwhelmingly supported holding elections within a year. While Maduro is gone, the regime he led remains, even though Rodriguez has dropped the anti-American rhetoric and has publicly displayed an openness to working with U.S. officials.

At Miami's airport, Venezuelan journalists in exile pressed Plasencia about the possibility of returning to Venezuela and the more than 450 political prisoners still held here.

"I think we should focus on the new and leave behind negative rhetoric, the malice, and the effort to deceive — to keep deceiving Venezuelans and the world," he said. Plasencia said, "Venezuelans should be happy to have these connections," referring to the flights.

The directive to reopen the commercial flights came directly from Trump after the operation that snatched Maduro, who is in New York City facing drug-trafficking charges that he denies. In a Jan. 28 cabinet meeting, Trump instructed Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy and U.S. military officials to reopen commercial airspace over Venezuela by the end of the day.

The turnaround was unusually fast for an international market that had been closed for so long to U.S. carriers. While it usually would have taken six months to reopen such a route, American Airlines worked with U.S. and Venezuelan officials to do so in half the time, said José Freig, vice president of international operations for the airline. "This comeback is a record," he said.

Starting in mid-May, American Airlines will increase its service to two daily flights between Miami and Caracas.

Iris Suárez was pleased that she could travel to Miami on the same flight that had just arrived. "Good things are happening," she said. "The flight was expensive but I prefer to pay for a direct flight that helps me avoid problems with connections and having to travel eight to 10 hours to get to the U.S."

During the three-hour flight Thursday to Caracas, U.S. and Venezuelan officials chatted in the aisles with businessmen, journalists and a handful of ordinary passengers, who nibbled on plantain chips.

While officials cast the resumption of flights as a way to reunite families, it won't eliminate the barriers that still make it difficult or risky for many Venezuelans in the U.S. to travel home. Many Venezuelans and dual nationals face expired passports that are difficult to renew after years of disrupted Venezuelan consular services in the U.S.

Levine Cava, the Miami-Dade mayor, said her Venezuelan constituents — Miami has the largest Venezuelan exile community in the U.S. — fear returning to a country where the same repressive regime remains in power.

"We are very, very anxious to see real progress towards democratization, " she told The Wall Street Journal. "We're happy that something is moving forward, but at this point, we don't see that the voices of Venezuelans are reflected in the selection of the leadership. We don't see exactly the path towards moving in that direction, and that is what this community here is seeking."

Many Venezuelans are living in the U.S. under temporary or contested immigration protections, making it risky to leave the country. And for many Venezuelans looking to travel in the other direction, Trump's travel ban still partially suspends visas for the country's citizens.

U.S. citizens also continue to face serious travel warnings. While the State Department lowered its Venezuela advisory in March to Level 3 — Reconsider Travel — it still warns of crime, kidnapping, terrorism, and poor health infrastructure while keeping several parts of the country under "Do Not Travel" guidance.

Write to

data.title: U.S. to Remove Tariffs on Scotch Whisky After King Charles-Trump Meeting — WSJ
data.description:
By Gavin Bade

The U.S. will remove tariffs on whisky from the United Kingdom, the Trump administration announced.

President Trump posted on social media that King Charles III persuaded him to lift the levies. "The King and Queen got me to do something that nobody else was able to do, without hardly even asking!" Trump posted.

Tariffs on Scotch whisky from the U.K. currently stand at 10%, but were slated to increase to 25% in June, when a previous trade compromise on the spirits was set to expire.

The "United States will allow preferential duty access for whiskey produced in the United Kingdom," U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said in a Thursday statement, "along with preferential treatment for other American and British goods."

Distillers and alcohol sellers on both sides of the Atlantic cheered the announcement.

"This is great news for our scotch whisky industry, which is worth almost GBP1bn in exports and supports thousands of jobs across the UK," said U.K. Business and Trade Secretary Peter Kyle. One billion British pounds is roughly equivalent to $1.3 billion.

"For months, many have worked tirelessly to return zero-for-zero tariff trade for whisky and bourbon," said Mark Kent, CEO of the Scotch Whisky Association.

The Scotch Whisky Association says that the U.K. exported about $1.3 billion worth of whisky to the U.S. in 2025, but in February said that its trade to the U.S. fell 15% after Trump's second-term tariffs.

"The removal of the 10% tariff on UK whisky would be a major victory for American hospitality businesses that are deeply impacted by international trade," said Chris Swonger CEO of the Distilled Spirits Council of the U.S.

Write to Gavin Bade at gavin.bade@wsj.com

data.title: AZN Stock Rise As Wall Street Dismisses FDA Committee’s Rejection Of Breast Cancer Drug
data.description:
Key points:
  • Jefferies analyst Michael Leuchten maintained a ‘Buy’ rating and $243 price target on AstraZeneca following the committee vote on camizestrant.
  • This suggests that the FDA is now unlikely to approve the drug in this setting, the analyst noted.
  • However, "this should come as no surprise," the analyst said.

Shares of AstraZeneca (AZN) closed up 1% on Thursday after Wall Street dismissed the negative vote on the company’s breast cancer drug as unsurprising.

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s Oncologic Drugs Advisory Committee (ODAC) voted 6-3 against a favorable benefit-risk profile for AstraZeneca’s camizestrant in combination with other drugs for the treatment of certain patients with advanced breast cancer, AstraZeneca said.

The FDA accepted AstraZeneca's application for camizestrant in July 2025 based on the results of a late-stage trial, which showed a 56% reduction in the risk of disease progression or death with the camizestrant combination versus standard-of-care treatment.

The FDA does not have to abide by the committee’s opinion, but takes its advice into consideration, the company noted. AstraZeneca’s application continues to be under the agency’s review.

Regulatory applications for camizestrant in this setting are also under review in the EU, Japan, and several other countries, AstraZeneca noted.

Analyst Chimes In

Jefferies analyst Michael Leuchten maintained a ‘Buy’ rating and $243 price target on AstraZeneca following the committee vote on camizestrant. This suggests that the FDA is now unlikely to approve the drug in this setting, the analyst noted, while adding that "this should come as no surprise" after the publication of the prior briefing documents.

The analyst noted that the trial in question is not part of the firm's ‘Buy’ thesis, and the trial accounts for "only a small part of the $80B 2030 sales target." The company reported total revenue of $58.74 billion in 2025.

Jefferies concluded that any weakness following this news "presents a buying opportunity."

How Did AZN Retail Traders React?

On Stocktwits, retail sentiment around AZN rose from ‘neutral’ to ‘bullish’ territory over the past 24 hours, while message volume remained at ‘high’ levels.

AZN stock has gained 29% over the past 12 months.

data.title: First Solar's quarterly sales rise on higher solar panel demand
data.description:
First Solar , the biggest U.S.-based solar panel maker, on Thursday reported higher quarterly sales that met Wall Street expectations, helped by demand from third parties and from India.

U.S. tariffs and stricter trade enforcement that raised the cost of imported solar panels have strengthened First Solar's pricing power, as developers increasingly turned to domestic suppliers.

Shares of the company rose 0.55% in extended trading.

“We delivered a strong start to 2026, with record first-quarter revenue, record sales in India, meaningful margin expansion, and Adjusted EBITDA above the top end of our first quarter preview range,” CEO Mark Widmar said in a statement.

First Solar posted first-quarter net sales of $1.04 billion, compared with $844.6 million a year ago, and in line with analysts' expectations, according to data compiled by LSEG.

It reported a net income of $346.6 million for the quarter ended March 31, up about 65.4% from the year-ago period.

The company said it expects second-quarter module sales of 3.4 to 4.0 gigawatts and adjusted EBITDA of $400 million to $500 million.

Last week, the U.S. Commerce Department unveiled preliminary antidumping duties on solar cells and panels from India, Indonesia and Laos, backing domestic manufacturers’ claims that producers in those countries sold products at unfairly low prices, adding to a decade-long series of tariffs on Asian solar imports.

data.title: SEM: Revenue up 5% but margins and net income fell; merger expected mid-2026 amid cost pressures
data.description:
Revenue grew 5% year-over-year to $1.42 billion, but net income and margins declined due to higher labor and operating costs. The pending merger to take the company private is expected to close mid-2026, with regulatory and inflationary risks remaining significant.

data.title: Dynamic Aerospace Systems Secures $275,000 Convertible Note From Labrys Fund II, Adds Warrants
data.description:
Dynamic Aerospace Systems entered into a convertible promissory note financing to bolster liquidity. The $275,000 note with an 8% one-time interest charge carries an original issue discount of $25,000 and $4,000 in fees, yielding $246,000 in net proceeds. It is convertible at the holder’s option at the lower of $0.50 or 80% of the lowest 15-day VWAP and matures on Apr 27, 2027. The company also issued 20,000 commitment shares and five-year warrants for 199,275 shares at a $0.65 exercise price.

Agreement details:

  • Agreement type: Convertible promissory note with equity incentives
  • Counterparty: Labrys Fund II
  • Signed / Effective: Apr 28 2026 / same
  • Duration / Termination: Through Apr 27 2027
  • Reason: Enhance liquidity for operations and growth

Original SEC Filing: DYNAMIC AEROSPACE SYSTEMS Corp [ BRQL ] - 8-K - Apr. 30, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.
